source: ACS 5-Year Estimates, aggregated to neighborhood/area level

clt-uptown.name: Uptown
clt-uptown.blurb: Charlotte's downtown core and central business district. Home to the Spectrum Center, Blumenthal Performing Arts, and the bulk of the city's Fortune 500 headquarters. A wave of luxury apartment towers has transformed Uptown into a true live-work neighborhood. The light rail connects residents to South End, the airport, and UNC Charlotte. Residents tend to be young professionals drawn by walkability and proximity to corporate offices.
clt-uptown.new_construction_note: Active — several mixed-use towers under construction along Stonewall and Trade corridors
clt-uptown.resale_activity: High — strong condo turnover driven by relocating professionals

clt-south-end.name: South End
clt-south-end.blurb: Charlotte's hottest neighborhood for the past decade. The Lynx Blue Line light rail runs through its center, and the Rail Trail provides a walking and biking spine lined with breweries, restaurants, and boutiques. Once an industrial corridor, South End has been almost entirely rebuilt with luxury apartments, creative office space, and ground-floor retail. It attracts a young, social crowd — median age is among the lowest in the metro.
clt-south-end.new_construction_note: Very active — one of the fastest-building neighborhoods in the Southeast
clt-south-end.resale_activity: Very high — rapid turnover as renters convert to buyers

clt-noda.name: NoDa
clt-noda.blurb: North Davidson — Charlotte's arts district. Galleries, live music venues, and street murals define this walkable neighborhood centered on North Davidson Street. Originally a textile mill village, NoDa preserved its cottage-style homes even as craft breweries and creative businesses moved in. The Blue Line Extension brought a light rail stop, accelerating development. You'll find a mix of longtime artist residents, young renters, and families drawn to the community feel.
clt-noda.new_construction_note: Moderate — infill townhomes and apartments near the light rail stop
clt-noda.resale_activity: Strong — renovated bungalows sell quickly, often above asking

clt-plaza-midwood.name: Plaza Midwood
clt-plaza-midwood.blurb: An eclectic, independently-minded neighborhood just east of Uptown. Central Avenue is lined with international restaurants, vintage shops, dive bars, and locally-owned cafés. Plaza Midwood has long been Charlotte's counterculture hub, though rising prices are shifting the demographic. The 1920s bungalows and tree-lined streets give it a distinctly older-Charlotte feel. One of the few inner-city neighborhoods where you'll see a taco truck next to a craft cocktail bar.
clt-plaza-midwood.new_construction_note: Limited — mostly renovation and some townhome infill
clt-plaza-midwood.resale_activity: Very strong — high demand and limited inventory keep prices climbing

clt-dilworth.name: Dilworth
clt-dilworth.blurb: Charlotte's first streetcar suburb and one of its most desirable addresses. Tree-canopied streets, Craftsman bungalows, and Latta Park anchor a neighborhood that's been upscale for over a century. East Boulevard and South Boulevard provide walkable restaurant and retail. Dilworth families fill the parks on weekends. The neighborhood association is fiercely protective of the area's character.
clt-dilworth.new_construction_note: Limited — teardowns replaced by larger builds, overall stock stable
clt-dilworth.resale_activity: Strong — homes sell fast and prices consistently outpace the metro

clt-myers-park.name: Myers Park
clt-myers-park.blurb: Charlotte's premier established-wealth neighborhood. Designed by John Nolen in the 1910s with curving, tree-lined boulevards. Features some of the largest and most expensive homes in the metro. Queens University sits at its heart. Myers Park residents tend to be established families and executives. Retail along Providence Road is upscale but understated. This is old Charlotte money.
clt-myers-park.new_construction_note: Minimal — mostly renovations and estate-scale teardown rebuilds
clt-myers-park.resale_activity: Moderate — lower turnover, but homes command premium prices

clt-elizabeth.name: Elizabeth
clt-elizabeth.blurb: A walkable, urban neighborhood bordering Uptown anchored by Novant Health Presbyterian Medical Center. Elizabeth attracts hospital workers, young professionals, and couples wanting an easy commute without the Myers Park price tag. Independence Park provides green space, and Elizabeth Avenue has solid brunch spots. Housing ranges from apartments near the hospital to renovated bungalows.
clt-elizabeth.new_construction_note: Moderate — townhome and apartment infill near the medical district
clt-elizabeth.resale_activity: Healthy — steady demand from hospital employees and Uptown commuters

clt-montford.name: Montford
clt-montford.blurb: A quiet residential area south of Elizabeth. Montford has a neighborhood-in-transition feel — older ranch homes sit next to new townhome developments. More affordable than adjacent Dilworth or Myers Park while still close to everything. Younger buyers are discovering Montford as pricier neighborhoods become out of reach.
clt-montford.new_construction_note: Growing — townhome clusters replacing older single-family lots
clt-montford.resale_activity: Active — strong demand from first-time buyers

clt-wesley-heights.name: Wesley Heights
clt-wesley-heights.blurb: An up-and-coming neighborhood west of Uptown with rich African-American heritage. Johnson C. Smith University anchors the southern end. Housing is diverse — grand Victorians, modest bungalows, new construction. Restaurants and breweries are popping up along West Trade Street. Proximity to Uptown makes it walkable for commuters, and prices remain below east-side neighborhoods.
clt-wesley-heights.new_construction_note: Active — significant new development along W. Trade Street
clt-wesley-heights.resale_activity: Growing — increasing investor and owner-occupant interest

clt-eastway-shamrock.name: Eastway / Shamrock
clt-eastway-shamrock.blurb: One of Charlotte's most diverse corridors along Eastway Drive and Central Avenue. International food is the highlight — Vietnamese pho shops, Mexican taquerias, Halal markets. Shamrock Drive has mid-century ranch homes on large lots. Prices are noticeably lower, making this attractive to first-time buyers. Community gardens and cultural festivals reflect the multicultural identity.
clt-eastway-shamrock.new_construction_note: Light — some apartment projects but largely existing stock
clt-eastway-shamrock.resale_activity: Moderate — affordable price point drives steady demand

clt-university.name: University Area
clt-university.blurb: Centered on UNC Charlotte, this fast-growing area has transformed from college-town fringe into a major hub. University Research Park houses thousands of tech and financial services jobs. The Blue Line Extension connects the area to Uptown. Student rentals dominate near campus, but new subdivisions are spreading. Retail is mostly chain-oriented along W.T. Harris Boulevard.
clt-university.new_construction_note: Very active — large-scale development along the light rail corridor
clt-university.resale_activity: High — strong demand from young professionals and investors

clt-ballantyne.name: Ballantyne
clt-ballantyne.blurb: A master-planned suburban community in south Charlotte functioning almost as its own city. Ballantyne Corporate Park is a major employment center. The Golf Club is the centerpiece, surrounded by upscale neighborhoods and top-rated schools. Families relocating to Charlotte frequently land here for schools and suburban lifestyle. The trade-off is distance — 30+ minutes to Uptown without traffic.
clt-ballantyne.new_construction_note: Active — new luxury subdivisions along Johnston and Rea Road corridors
clt-ballantyne.resale_activity: Strong — family-driven demand keeps resale competitive

clt-lake-norman.name: Lake Norman East
clt-lake-norman.blurb: Three distinct lakeside towns along I-77 north of Charlotte. Davidson is a charming college town with walkable Main Street and strict architectural standards. Cornelius offers waterfront dining and mixed development. Huntersville is the largest and most suburban with big-box retail and master-planned communities like Birkdale Village. All three offer lake access, excellent schools, and a 20-40 minute drive to Uptown.
clt-lake-norman.new_construction_note: Very active — rapid growth in all three towns
clt-lake-norman.resale_activity: Strong — especially for lakefront and lake-access properties

clt-north-iredell.name: Mooresville / Statesville
clt-north-iredell.blurb: North of Lake Norman, Mooresville calls itself Race City USA. The town has grown rapidly with affordable new-construction subdivisions. Downtown Mooresville has a revitalizing Main Street. Statesville is the Iredell County seat with traditional small-town feel. Sherrills Ford offers large-lot lakefront living. The trade-off is a longer commute — 40-60 minutes to Uptown.
clt-north-iredell.new_construction_note: Very active — Mooresville is one of the fastest-growing towns in NC
clt-north-iredell.resale_activity: Strong — affordable prices drive high turnover

clt-cabarrus.name: Concord / Kannapolis
clt-cabarrus.blurb: Northeast of Charlotte in Cabarrus County. Concord is known for Charlotte Motor Speedway and Concord Mills mall. Downtown Concord has a walkable historic district. Kannapolis is undergoing revival anchored by the new baseball stadium and research campus. Housing is significantly more affordable than Mecklenburg County.
clt-cabarrus.new_construction_note: Very active — especially along I-85 and near new Kannapolis downtown
clt-cabarrus.resale_activity: High — affordability relative to Charlotte drives strong demand

clt-matthews-mint-hill.name: Matthews / Mint Hill
clt-matthews-mint-hill.blurb: Two suburban towns in southeast Mecklenburg County with small-town atmosphere. Downtown Matthews has an active Main Street with local shops and a farmers market. Mint Hill is more residential with large-lot homes. Both have good CMS schools and easier commutes to Uptown than southern or northern suburbs.
clt-matthews-mint-hill.new_construction_note: Moderate — infill in Matthews, larger subdivisions in Mint Hill

clt-indian-trail-stallings.name: Indian Trail / Stallings
clt-indian-trail-stallings.blurb: Fast-growing Union County towns southeast of Charlotte. A magnet for young families seeking new construction, larger lots, and top-rated Union County Public Schools. Master-planned communities offer pools, trails, and clubhouses. Car-dependent but the price-to-space ratio draws buyers from across the metro. Indian Trail's population has tripled since 2000.
clt-indian-trail-stallings.new_construction_note: Very active — among the highest new single-family construction rates
clt-indian-trail-stallings.resale_activity: Strong — schools drive consistent family demand

clt-gastonia.name: Gastonia
clt-gastonia.blurb: Gaston County's largest city west of Charlotte along I-85. Downtown is mid-revitalization with new restaurants and loft apartments in converted mills. Belmont has emerged as a charming small-town alternative with vibrant Main Street. The appeal is price — homes cost a fraction of Mecklenburg County, and the commute to Uptown is 25-35 minutes.
clt-gastonia.new_construction_note: Growing — affordable land attracting builders, especially Belmont
clt-gastonia.resale_activity: Moderate — affordable price point keeps inventory moving

ral-downtown.name: Downtown Raleigh
ral-downtown.blurb: Heart of North Carolina's capital. Fayetteville Street is the central corridor — pedestrian-friendly and lined with restaurants. The area around City Market and Moore Square has seen massive mixed-use development. Raleigh's tech economy (Red Hat, Citrix, Pendo) and state government keep the workforce diverse. First Friday art walks and a strong local food scene give downtown real energy.
ral-downtown.new_construction_note: Very active — multiple high-rise towers under construction
ral-downtown.resale_activity: High — strong condo market with rapid turnover

ral-north-hills.name: North Hills
ral-north-hills.blurb: Raleigh's premier mixed-use district built around the Midtown development by Kane Realty. Functions as a second downtown — office towers, luxury apartments, restaurants, a movie theater, and greenway all within walking distance. Attracts affluent professionals. Six Forks Road provides I-440 access. Surrounding residential areas feature established homes increasingly being renovated or replaced.
ral-north-hills.new_construction_note: Very active — continued Midtown expansion with new towers and retail
ral-north-hills.resale_activity: Strong — high demand for both new and existing homes

ral-five-points.name: Five Points / Hayes Barton
ral-five-points.blurb: One of Raleigh's most sought-after neighborhoods centered on the five-way intersection of Glenwood, Fairview, and Whitaker Mill. Hayes Barton is the residential heart — stately 1920s-40s homes on wide tree-lined streets. Local favorites like Lilly's Pizza anchor the commercial node. A mix of established families, professionals, and empty nesters who value walkability and character.
ral-five-points.new_construction_note: Minimal — historic preservation limits new builds
ral-five-points.resale_activity: Very strong — limited inventory and premium prices

ral-oakwood-mordecai.name: Oakwood / Mordecai
ral-oakwood-mordecai.blurb: Raleigh's premier historic districts. Historic Oakwood is one of the largest intact Victorian neighborhoods in the South with homes dating to the 1870s. Both neighborhoods attract history enthusiasts willing to invest in older homes. The annual Oakwood Candlelight Tour is a Raleigh holiday tradition. Walking distance to Person Street restaurants is a big draw.
ral-oakwood-mordecai.new_construction_note: Very limited — historic district overlay restricts new builds
ral-oakwood-mordecai.resale_activity: Moderate — low inventory but homes sell at premium

ral-cameron-village.name: Cameron Village
ral-cameron-village.blurb: Named for one of the first open-air shopping centers in the Southeast (1949, recently renovated). The surrounding neighborhood has a mix of mid-century ranches, bungalows, and new infill. Central inside-the-beltline location is highly desirable for professionals working downtown or at NC State. Hillsborough Street gives the southern edge a younger vibe.
ral-cameron-village.new_construction_note: Moderate — townhome infill plus shopping center renovation
ral-cameron-village.resale_activity: Strong — inside-the-beltline location keeps demand high

ral-glenwood-south.name: Glenwood South
ral-glenwood-south.blurb: Raleigh's nightlife and dining district along Glenwood Avenue west of downtown. The strip of bars, restaurants, and clubs draws crowds on weekends. Residential side has grown with mid-rise apartments and condos. Walkable, vibrant, and skews young. Residents tolerate weekend noise for the convenience of dozens of dining options at their doorstep.
ral-glenwood-south.new_construction_note: Active — continued apartment and mixed-use development
ral-glenwood-south.resale_activity: High — popular with young professionals and investors

ral-boylan-heights.name: Boylan Heights
ral-boylan-heights.blurb: Compact, walkable historic neighborhood on a hill south of downtown with panoramic skyline views from Boylan Avenue Bridge. Early 1900s bungalows on a tight grid. Popular with young families who want a short walk to downtown. The Dorothea Dix Park redevelopment — Raleigh's answer to Central Park — borders the neighborhood and will be transformative.
ral-boylan-heights.new_construction_note: Very limited — historic district, mostly renovations
ral-boylan-heights.resale_activity: Strong — Dix Park proximity driving increasing demand

ral-warehouse.name: Warehouse District
ral-warehouse.blurb: Raleigh's creative economy hub south of downtown. Converted warehouses house galleries, design studios, breweries, and tech startups. Residential development is newer — loft-style apartments and condos. Walkable to downtown and future Dix Park. Industrial-chic aesthetic attracts artists, designers, and creative professionals.
ral-warehouse.new_construction_note: Active — continued adaptive reuse and new residential
ral-warehouse.resale_activity: Moderate — small market but growing buyer interest

ral-cary.name: Cary
ral-cary.blurb: The Triangle's premier suburb, consistently ranked among the best places to live in the US for excellent schools, low crime, and manicured greenways. Downtown Cary is experiencing revival. Morrisville has one of the most diverse populations in the state — heavily influenced by South Asian communities drawn to RTP tech jobs. Suburban and car-dependent, but greenways provide excellent walking and biking.
ral-cary.new_construction_note: Active — downtown Cary redevelopment plus suburban expansion west
ral-cary.resale_activity: Very strong — top-rated schools keep family demand high

ral-apex.name: Apex
ral-apex.blurb: Voted America's Best Place to Live by Money magazine. Historic downtown on Salem Street is genuinely charming — local breweries, restaurants, and shops. New development extends south into former farmland. Apex families benefit from Wake County schools and a 20-minute RTP commute. Growth has strained roads but new schools and parks have followed.
ral-apex.new_construction_note: Very active — one of the fastest-growing towns in Wake County
ral-apex.resale_activity: Very strong — high demand across all price points

ral-holly-springs.name: Holly Springs / Fuquay-Varina
ral-holly-springs.blurb: Two rapidly growing towns in southwest Wake County. Holly Springs has attracted biotech companies including a major Novartis plant. Fuquay-Varina's downtown has a thriving brewery and restaurant scene. Both offer affordable new construction compared to Cary and Apex, making them popular with young families.
ral-holly-springs.new_construction_note: Very active — among the fastest-growing in the state
ral-holly-springs.resale_activity: Strong — affordable price points attract steady interest

ral-wake-forest.name: Wake Forest
ral-wake-forest.blurb: Historic college town northeast of Raleigh that's become one of the Triangle's fastest-growing areas. A walkable downtown, strong schools, and new development along US-1 attract families. Rolesville has grown from rural crossroads into a bedroom community with new subdivisions.
ral-wake-forest.new_construction_note: Very active — growth radiating along US-1 and Capital corridors
ral-wake-forest.resale_activity: Strong — schools and relative affordability drive demand

ral-garner.name: Garner
ral-garner.blurb: Directly south of Raleigh, long overshadowed by trendier neighbors but offers some of the best value in the metro. White Deer Park and Lake Benson are natural amenities. Housing is a mix of established subdivisions and newer construction on the town's southern edge. Short commute to downtown Raleigh.
ral-garner.new_construction_note: Growing — new subdivisions and infill near downtown

ral-knightdale-wendell.name: Knightdale / Wendell
ral-knightdale-wendell.blurb: East of Raleigh along US-64, the metro's next growth frontier. Knightdale has boomed with new development. Wendell retains more small-town feel but is growing fast. Both benefit from I-540 and US-64 access to downtown Raleigh. Home prices are among the most affordable in Wake County.
ral-knightdale-wendell.new_construction_note: Very active — some of the most affordable new construction in Wake County
ral-knightdale-wendell.resale_activity: Strong — affordability and new-home buyers drive turnover

ral-clayton.name: Clayton / Smithfield
ral-clayton.blurb: In Johnston County southeast of Raleigh, Clayton has transformed from a small farming town into one of the Triangle's explosive growth areas. The I-40 corridor provides commuter access to Raleigh and RTP, while home prices remain well below Wake County levels. The historic downtown is developing with new restaurants and shops. Master-planned communities with amenity centers dot the landscape. Clayton's growth rate — over 50% in the past decade — shows no sign of slowing as Triangle housing costs push buyers further from the core.
ral-clayton.new_construction_note: Extremely active — one of the fastest-growing municipalities in NC
ral-clayton.resale_activity: High — affordable prices and Johnston County schools drive demand

clt-southpark.name: SouthPark
clt-southpark.blurb: Charlotte's premier retail and office district south of Myers Park. SouthPark Mall is the city's top luxury shopping destination with Nordstrom, Neiman Marcus, and high-end boutiques. The surrounding neighborhoods range from 1970s-era condos to gated estate communities. Phillips Place and Morrison add walkable dining clusters. Corporate offices for major firms line Carnegie and Fairview. SouthPark attracts established professionals and families wanting top schools with a suburban feel minutes from Uptown.
clt-southpark.new_construction_note: Moderate — luxury condos and apartment communities near the mall, limited single-family
clt-southpark.resale_activity: Strong — consistent demand for established neighborhoods near top schools

clt-west-lake-norman.name: Western Lake Norman
clt-west-lake-norman.blurb: The western shore of Lake Norman in Lincoln and Catawba Counties. Denver has grown from a rural crossroads into a popular lakeside community with new subdivisions, restaurants, and a revitalizing downtown. Sherrills Ford offers large-lot lakefront living with more of a country feel. Terrell anchors the northern end. The appeal is lake access at lower prices than the eastern shore towns like Cornelius and Davidson. Schools are in the Lincoln County system. The commute to Charlotte is longer — 40-50 minutes — but the lifestyle trade-off works for many families.
clt-west-lake-norman.new_construction_note: Very active — affordable lakeside lots drawing builders, especially in Denver
clt-west-lake-norman.resale_activity: Strong — lakefront and lake-access properties move quickly

clt-belmont-mt-holly.name: Belmont / Mount Holly
clt-belmont-mt-holly.blurb: Two Gaston County towns east of Gastonia along the Catawba River. Belmont has emerged as one of the Charlotte metro's most charming small towns — a vibrant Main Street with restaurants, breweries, and boutiques draws visitors from across the region. Mount Holly is more working-class but growing, with river access and a developing downtown. Both are significantly more affordable than Mecklenburg County with a 20-minute commute to Uptown Charlotte via I-85. The Belmont Main Street scene has made the town especially popular with young couples and families.
clt-belmont-mt-holly.new_construction_note: Active — Belmont especially is attracting new townhome and single-family development
clt-belmont-mt-holly.resale_activity: Strong — Belmont's Main Street appeal drives consistent demand

clt-lake-wylie.name: Lake Wylie
clt-lake-wylie.blurb: A lake community straddling the NC-SC border southwest of Charlotte. Lake Wylie — the body of water — draws boaters, anglers, and families who want waterfront living without Lake Norman prices. The SC side (York County) has grown rapidly with master-planned communities, many with lake access and resort-style amenities. Clover to the west adds small-town charm. South Carolina's lower taxes and Clover School District's strong reputation attract Charlotte commuters. The Buster Boyd Bridge provides a 25-minute drive to Uptown.
clt-lake-wylie.new_construction_note: Very active — large subdivisions with lake access are the primary driver
clt-lake-wylie.resale_activity: Strong — waterfront properties command premiums and sell fast

clt-fort-mill.name: Fort Mill / Tega Cay
clt-fort-mill.blurb: Just across the South Carolina border, Fort Mill has become one of the Charlotte metro's most sought-after suburbs. Lower SC taxes, excellent Fort Mill School District, and master-planned communities like Baxter and Riverwalk attract droves of relocating families. Downtown Fort Mill has a growing restaurant and shop scene. Tega Cay is a small lakeside city on Lake Wylie with a tight-knit community feel. Indian Land to the east is newer development — subdivision after subdivision of affordable new construction. The I-77 commute to Uptown is 20-30 minutes without traffic, though rush hour congestion is notorious.
clt-fort-mill.new_construction_note: Extremely active — Fort Mill and Indian Land are among the fastest-growing areas in the entire Southeast
clt-fort-mill.resale_activity: Very strong — school district reputation keeps family demand intense

ral-durham.name: Durham
ral-durham.blurb: Durham has reinvented itself from a tobacco and textile city into a nationally recognized food, tech, and arts destination. Downtown Durham — the American Tobacco Campus, Brightleaf Square, and the Durham Performing Arts Center — anchors a vibrant urban core. Duke University and Duke Health are the largest employers. The food scene rivals cities twice its size, with multiple James Beard nominees. Durham's neighborhoods range from historic Walltown and Old North Durham to booming developments near South Point and Southpoint Mall. RTP (Research Triangle Park) sits between Durham and Raleigh, housing major tech and pharma companies.
ral-durham.new_construction_note: Very active — downtown mixed-use, plus large suburban subdivisions in south Durham
ral-durham.resale_activity: Strong — Duke-driven demand plus tech workers keep the market competitive

ral-harnett.name: Harnett County
ral-harnett.blurb: Rural Harnett County south of Raleigh is the Triangle's affordability frontier. Lillington is the county seat, a small town centered on the Cape Fear River. Angier, closer to the Wake County line, has seen the most growth as commuters discover its low home prices and country feel. Fort Liberty (formerly Fort Bragg) to the south brings military families. The trade-off is a longer commute — 45-60 minutes to Raleigh — and fewer amenities. But for buyers priced out of Wake County, Harnett offers new-construction homes at half the price.
ral-harnett.new_construction_note: Growing — affordable land attracting national builders, especially near Angier
ral-harnett.resale_activity: Moderate — price-driven demand from Triangle commuters

ral-lee.name: Lee County / Sanford
ral-lee.blurb: Lee County sits at the western edge of the Triangle's growth wave, centered on Sanford. The big story is the Vineyard Wind-adjacent clean energy boom — major manufacturers including Toyota, Wolfspeed, and VinFast have announced facilities in or near Lee County, promising thousands of jobs. Downtown Sanford has a charming brick-lined Main Street with breweries and restaurants. The area is still genuinely affordable compared to Wake County. As the Triangle's job base shifts westward, Sanford is positioned for significant growth over the next decade.
ral-lee.new_construction_note: Accelerating — manufacturing announcements driving residential interest and builder attention
ral-lee.resale_activity: Growing — still affordable but prices rising as awareness increases

ral-johnston-south.name: Johnston County South
ral-johnston-south.blurb: Southern Johnston County beyond Clayton, centered on the county seat of Smithfield. This is outlet mall country — the Carolina Premium Outlets draw shoppers from across the state. Smithfield has a small downtown with the Ava Gardner Museum and a growing restaurant scene. Selma sits at the I-95/I-40 junction, making it a crossroads town. Benson and Four Oaks to the south retain a rural, agricultural character. The entire area is significantly more affordable than northern Johnston County and Wake County, attracting buyers who want land and space.
ral-johnston-south.new_construction_note: Moderate — some new subdivisions near Smithfield and Selma, but growth is slower than northern Johnston
ral-johnston-south.resale_activity: Steady — affordable prices attract buyers seeking value and acreage

clt-north-charlotte.name: North Charlotte / Druid Hills
clt-north-charlotte.blurb: A historically working-class area north of Uptown and west of NoDa that's seeing rapid change. Druid Hills, Seversville, and the surrounding blocks have deep roots in Charlotte's African-American community. The housing stock is mostly small bungalows and ranch homes from the mid-20th century — some well-maintained, others teardown targets for new construction. Proximity to Uptown and NoDa makes the area attractive to investors and first-time buyers looking for relative affordability close to the city center. New townhome and apartment projects are filling in vacant lots, and breweries and restaurants are starting to follow. The neighborhood is in transition — longtime residents live next door to new builds.
clt-north-charlotte.new_construction_note: Active — infill townhomes and apartments, significant teardown-rebuild activity
clt-north-charlotte.resale_activity: Growing — investor interest high, owner-occupant demand rising as nearby neighborhoods price up

clt-monroe.name: Monroe / Weddington
clt-monroe.blurb: The Union County seat and surrounding affluent towns southeast of Charlotte. Monroe is the commercial hub with a revitalizing downtown and more affordable housing. Weddington and Marvin are among the wealthiest communities in the metro — large-lot custom homes, horse farms, and top-rated schools in the Union County system. Waxhaw has a charming Main Street with local restaurants and shops. The entire corridor along US-74 and NC-16 has seen explosive residential growth. The trade-off is commute distance — 35-45 minutes to Uptown Charlotte — but families pay it for the schools and space.
clt-monroe.new_construction_note: Very active — large subdivisions in all four towns, especially along NC-16 corridor
clt-monroe.resale_activity: Strong — Union County schools and large lots drive consistent family demand

ral-rtp.name: Research Triangle Park
ral-rtp.blurb: The largest research park in the US, spanning 7,000 acres between Raleigh and Durham. Home to over 300 companies including Cisco, IBM, Fidelity, and Credit Suisse employing more than 50,000 people. RTP itself is mostly office and lab campuses — not residential — but the surrounding areas (particularly Morrisville and south Durham) have boomed with apartments, townhomes, and retail to serve the workforce. The Hub RTP mixed-use development is adding walkable dining and gathering spaces to what was once a car-dependent office park. RTP is the economic engine that drives the entire Triangle's growth and the reason tech workers relocate here from around the country.
ral-rtp.new_construction_note: Active — The Hub RTP development plus surrounding apartment communities
ral-rtp.resale_activity: Strong — proximity to major employers keeps demand high for nearby residential areas

ral-chapel-hill.name: Chapel Hill / Carrboro
ral-chapel-hill.blurb: A college town with a progressive identity centered on the University of North Carolina. Franklin Street is the iconic main drag — bookshops, restaurants, bars, and the kind of walkable energy you'd expect from a top-tier university town. Carrboro, adjacent to the west, has an artsy, independent streak with a thriving local food co-op, live music venues, and a farmer's market that's one of the best in the state. Housing ranges from student apartments near campus to multi-million-dollar homes in neighborhoods like Governors Club and Southern Village. Chapel Hill is more expensive than most Triangle communities and fiercely protective of its small-town character despite growing development pressure.
ral-chapel-hill.new_construction_note: Moderate — constrained by town growth limits, mostly infill and apartment projects near campus
ral-chapel-hill.resale_activity: Strong — UNC connection and walkability keep demand high despite premium prices
